          Didi VIEsIn my old life, I was a derivatives structurer at an inv=
estment bank, and an experience that I frequently had was getting calls lik=
e this:Relationship banker: Our client wants to do ______.Me: That=E2=80=99=
s illegal.Relationship banker: Yes that=E2=80=99s our problem.Me: Ah.Relati=
onship banker: We were hoping there was a =E2=80=A6 derivative?Sometimes th=
ere was! If you came to me and said =E2=80=9Cour client would like to buy 8=
% of a public company, but not disclose her ownership,=E2=80=9D I would say=
 =E2=80=9Cwell, under U.S. law you do have to disclose that level of stock =
ownership, but I could put you in a nice derivative that gets around that r=
equirement.=E2=80=9D The client could enter into a swap that gives her econ=
omic exposure to the shares, but not actual ownership, so she would not be =
subject to the laws about ownership disclosure. Everyone is happy, more or =
less. (No one is happy, and we have  talked a  few  times about how mad eve=
ryone is that Archegos Capital Management=C2=A0was allowed to do this.)So I=
 have a soft spot for Chinese VIEs. The idea is that, under Chinese law, it=
 is somewhere between =E2=80=9Ccomplicated=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cforbidden=
=E2=80=9D for foreigners to own certain big important Chinese tech=C2=A0com=
panies. This is a problem for those companies if they want to raise capital=
 from foreign investors and list their stocks on foreign stock exchanges. B=
ut there is a solution. =E2=80=9COwnership=E2=80=9D of a company is a compl=
icated notion, a vague jumble of rights to elect directors and approve merg=
ers and claim a residual interest=C2=A0in the company=E2=80=99s cash flows.=
 You could break those things up and sell them separately. Write a profit-s=
haring contract that says =E2=80=9CA will pay B all of A=E2=80=99s profits =
after expenses for the next 100 years, renewable at B=E2=80=99s option,=E2=
=80=9D and hey that=E2=80=99s a residual claim on cash flows. (Or something=
 vaguer: =E2=80=9CA will pay B an annual consulting fee that B decides in i=
ts total discretion based on the economic value of the relationship,=E2=80=
=9D etc.; not technically a residual claim but what else is it?) =E2=80=9CB=
 will provide management services to A and A will follow B=E2=80=99s instru=
ctions,=E2=80=9D hey that=E2=80=99s basically control. =E2=80=9CB will have=
 the right to appoint a majority of A=E2=80=99s board of directors,=E2=80=
=9D put it in a contract, it=E2=80=99s not actually stock ownership. Etc. W=
rite some contracts that, bundled together, look like ownership, but=C2=A0a=
ren=E2=80=99t=C2=A0ownership.With Chinese companies this sort of thing is g=
enerally called a =E2=80=9Cvariable interest entity.=E2=80=9D You set up a =
company in the Cayman Islands that can be owned by anyone. The Caymans comp=
any enters into a series of contracts with the local Chinese company, givin=
g it, not ownership, but certain carefully curated economic interests and c=
ontrol rights over the Chinese company. Then you list the Caymans company i=
n the U.S., and people buy its stock, and they sort of pretend that they=E2=
=80=99re buying stock in the Chinese company =E2=80=94=C2=A0they sort of pr=
etend that the Chinese company is a=C2=A0subsidiary=C2=A0of the Caymans hol=
ding company =E2=80=94=C2=A0even though really they=E2=80=99re only buying =
an empty shell that has certain contractual relationships with the Chinese =
company.The problem with this is that it sort of sounds like you=E2=80=99re=
 kidding. So here is the prospectus for Didi Global Inc., a Cayman Islands =
company that has certain contractual relationships with a giant Chinese rid=
e-hailing company. (Technically the top-level Chinese company is called Bei=
jing Xiaoju Science and Technology Co. Ltd., though colloquially it is =E2=
=80=9CDidi Chuxing.=E2=80=9D) Didi Global did an initial public offering of=
 its American depositary shares last week; it has a market capitalization o=
f something like $57 billion. Page 12 of the prospectus has a diagram of th=
e corporate structure, which looks almost normal:Looks like a holding compa=
ny with some intermediate holding companies and operating subsidiaries, fin=
e. The only weird thing is that somewhere near the middle there is a=C2=A0d=
ouble=C2=A0arrow (representing =E2=80=9Ccontractual arrangements=E2=80=9D) =
rather than a single arrow (representing =E2=80=9Cequity interest=E2=80=9D)=
. Didi Global=E2=80=99s shareholders =E2=80=9Cown,=E2=80=9D in some fairly =
normal sense, everything above the double arrow, right down to one =E2=80=
=9Cwholly foreign owned enterprise=E2=80=9D (WFOE) in China. Everything bel=
ow the double arrow =E2=80=94=C2=A0the actual ride-hailing business, etc. =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0is slightly askew; they just have contractual rights to do s=
tuff with it.The actual contractual rights are spelled out on pages 100-102=
 of the prospectus under the heading =E2=80=9CContractual Arrangements with=
 Our Variable Interest Entities,=E2=80=9D and they are worth reading. =E2=
=80=9CAgreements that Allow Us to Receive Economic Benefits from Our Variab=
le Interest Entities=E2=80=9D is one sub-heading, describing a contract pro=
viding that =E2=80=9CBeijing DiDi or its designated parties have the exclus=
ive right to provide Xiaoju Technology with comprehensive technical support=
, consulting services and other services,=E2=80=9D and that =E2=80=9CXiaoju=
 Technology agrees to pay services fees, the amount of which is determined =
by Beijing DiDi on the basis of the work performed and commercial value of =
the services.=E2=80=9D Is that a residual claim on the cash flows of the ri=
de-hailing business? Maybe!=E2=80=9CAgreements that Provide Us with Effecti=
ve Control over Our Variable Interest Entities=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9CAgreem=
ents that Provide Us with the Option to Purchase the Equity Interest in Our=
 Variable Interest Entities=E2=80=9D are two other sub-headings. (The latte=
r is less of a stock option and more of a transfer restriction: It=E2=80=99=
s not that Didi can practically buy the shares in the VIE; it=E2=80=99s tha=
t it doesn=E2=80=99t want the shareholders selling those shares to someone =
else.) And here=E2=80=99s this:Spousal Consent Letters. The spouses of the =
shareholders of Xiaoju Technology have each signed a spousal consent letter=
 agreeing that the equity interests in Xiaoju Technology held by and regist=
ered under the name of the respective shareholders will be disposed pursuan=
t to the contractual agreements with Beijing DiDi. Each spouse agreed not t=
o assert any rights over the equity interest in Xiaoju Technology held by t=
he respective shareholder.Somebody=C2=A0owns the ride-hailing business, in =
a technical legal sense, and it=E2=80=99s not Didi Global or its shareholde=
rs. You don=E2=80=99t want the actual owners, or their spouses, to go aroun=
d selling the shares out from under Didi.I don=E2=80=99t want to pick on Di=
di. This is a standard method for mainland Chinese internet companies to go=
 public, Didi is just the latest in a long line of big companies to use it,=
 the market has come to accept it, and Didi=E2=80=99s Chinese counsel has o=
pined that =E2=80=9Cthe ownership structure of our principal variable inter=
est entity ... will not result in any violation of the applicable PRC laws =
or regulations currently in effect=E2=80=9D and that =E2=80=9Cthe agreement=
s under the contractual arrangement among Beijing DiDi, Xiaoju Technology a=
nd its shareholders are currently valid, binding and enforceable in accorda=
nce with their terms and the applicable PRC laws or regulations currently i=
n effect.=E2=80=9D It=E2=80=99s all fine!But, again, it sounds like you=E2=
=80=99re kidding, and as that legal opinion sort of implies, the laws can c=
hange.  So!Regulators in Beijing are planning rule changes that would allow=
 them to block a Chinese company from listing overseas even if the unit sel=
ling shares is incorporated outside China, closing a loophole long-used by =
the country=E2=80=99s technology giants, according to people familiar with =
the matter.The China Securities Regulatory Commission is leading efforts to=
 revise rules on overseas listings that have been in effect since 1994 and =
make no reference to companies registered in places like the Cayman Islands=
, said the people, asking not to be identified discussing a private matter.=
 Once amended, the rules would require firms structured using the so-called=
 Variable Interest Entity model to seek approval before going public in Hon=
g Kong or the U.S., the people said.=C2=A0The proposed change is the first =
indication of how Beijing plans to implement a crackdown on overseas listin=
gs flagged by the country=E2=80=99s State Council on Tuesday. Closer oversi=
ght would plug a gap that=E2=80=99s been used for two decades by technology=
 giants from Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. to Tencent Holdings Ltd. to attract=
 foreign capital and list offshore, potentially thwarting the ambitions of =
firms like ByteDance Ltd. contemplating going public outside the mainland. =
=E2=80=A6Pioneered by Sina Corp. and its investment bankers during a 2000 i=
nitial public offering, the VIE framework has never been formally endorsed =
by Beijing. It has nevertheless enabled Chinese companies to sidestep restr=
ictions on foreign investment in sensitive sectors including the Internet i=
ndustry. The structure allows a Chinese firm to transfer profits to an offs=
hore entity -- registered in places like the Cayman Islands or the British =
Virgin Islands -- with shares that foreign investors can then own.While vir=
tually every major Chinese internet company has used the structure, it=E2=
=80=99s become increasingly worrisome for Beijing as it tightens its grip o=
n technology firms that have infiltrated every corner of Chinese life and c=
ontrol reams of consumer data. Authorities so far have little legal recours=
e to prevent sensitive overseas listings, as with the recent Didi Global In=
c. IPO, which went ahead despite requests for a delay from regulators.The a=
dditional oversight could bestow a level of legitimacy on the VIE structure=
 that=E2=80=99s been a perennial worry for global investors given the shaky=
 legal ground on which it stands.Yeah that=E2=80=99s not the worst outcome.=
 The worst outcome would be something like the Chinese government declaring=
 =E2=80=9Call these VIE contracts are actually a disguised form of foreign =
ownership, which is not allowed by the rules, so they are all void and your=
 Didi and Alibaba shares are worthless.=E2=80=9D That would =E2=80=A6=C2=A0=
I mean, these VIE contracts=C2=A0are=C2=A0a disguised form of foreign owner=
ship, and a fairly thin disguise. (It=E2=80=99s not like Chinese government=
 authorities are unaware that foreign investors own shares in Alibaba; it=
=E2=80=99s just that they seem to accept this level of technical compliance=
 with the rules.) This is a real risk! It=E2=80=99s in Didi=E2=80=99s risk =
factors:PRC laws and regulations impose restrictions on foreign ownership a=
nd investment in certain internet-based businesses. We are an exempted comp=
any incorporated in the Cayman Islands and our PRC subsidiaries are conside=
red foreign-invested enterprises. To comply with PRC laws, regulations and =
regulatory requirements, we set up a series of contractual arrangements ent=
ered into among some of our PRC subsidiaries, our VIEs and their shareholde=
rs to conduct some of our operations in China. ...We have been further advi=
sed by our PRC legal counsel that there are substantial uncertainties regar=
ding the interpretation and application of current or future PRC laws and r=
egulations. Thus, the PRC government may ultimately take a view contrary to=
 the opinion of our PRC legal counsel. If the PRC government otherwise find=
 that we are in violation of any existing or future PRC laws or regulations=
 or lack the necessary permits or licenses to operate our business, the rel=
evant governmental authorities would have broad discretion in dealing with =
such violation, including, without limitation:=C2=A0=E2=80=A6revoking the b=
usiness licenses and/or operating licenses of our PRC entities; ...requirin=
g us to restructure our ownership structure or operations, including termin=
ating the contractual arrangements with our VIEs and deregistering the equi=
ty pledges of our VIEs, which in turn would affect our ability to consolida=
te, derive economic interests from, or exert effective control over our VIE=
s and their subsidiaries; ...If the Chinese government decides =E2=80=9CVIE=
s are okay, but you have to get our permission to list them abroad,=E2=80=
=9D that's not a worst-case scenario even if it never grants permission. Th=
e bad news would be if the ones already listed abroad were just, you know,=
=C2=A0voided.Anyway there is a whole mess of Chinese and U.S. government pr=
oclamations about cracking down on Chinese companies listing abroad. On the=
 Chinese side,=C2=A0=E2=80=9CDidi Plunges Below IPO Price as  China Crackdo=
wn Brings U.S. Pain,=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9CChina=E2=80=99s crackdown on US =
listings threatens $2tn market.=E2=80=9D And on the U.S. side: =E2=80=9CInv=
estment Review Panel Gets Wider Role Under Biden in Rivalry With China,=E2=
=80=9D and =E2=80=9CRepublican senator Marco Rubio lambasts =E2=80=98reckle=
ss and irresponsible=E2=80=99 Didi listing.=E2=80=9D If U.S. regulators ban=
 Chinese firms from listing in the U.S., and Chinese regulators also ban Ch=
inese firms from listing in the U.S., I guess it will be hard for Chinese f=
irms to list in the U.S. But maybe they=E2=80=99ll find a way anyway. Maybe=
 there=E2=80=99s a derivative.Palantir SPACsThe basic deal, when a company =
goes public, is that anyone can buy its stock. You put out a notice saying =
=E2=80=9Chey we have stock for sale, please buy it,=E2=80=9D and then whoev=
er is willing to pay the most buys the stock. The advantage of this, for th=
e company, is generally that you=C2=A0can raise a lot of money. Deep liquid=
 public markets are good for valuation; people are more willing to take a r=
isk on buying stock that they can easily resell. So if you want money, havi=
ng public stock is good. The disadvantage is that anyone can buy your stock=
. Activist investors can buy your stock and yell at you to change things. H=
ostile bidders can tender for your stock to try to take you over. Crazy ret=
ail investors can push your stock around. Index funds can buy your stock an=
d sit around doing nothing. Etc.[1]There is another model, in which you int=
erview=C2=A0the people who want to buy your stock and choose the best ones.=
 Price matters =E2=80=94=C2=A0people who will pay more for your stock are b=
etter for you than people who will pay less =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is not th=
e only thing. Buying your stock is a way to start a relationship, and you w=
ill try to sell stock to people who will be good relationship partners. Wis=
e investors who can give you good advice will be more appealing than hands-=
off index funds. Hands-off index funds will be more appealing than mean act=
ivists who will boss you around and try to fire your managers. Selling stoc=
k to your competitors seems bad. Selling stock to your suppliers or custome=
rs to cement your business relationships seems good.The second model is typ=
ical, though not universal, in venture-capital-funded private companies. VC=
s advertise themselves to startups as wise advisers, as helpful (but not in=
trusive) board members, as providers of operational expertise and introduct=
ions to customers and suppliers. This is so standard in venture capital tha=
t it is  faintly scandalous that Tiger Global, a big hedge fund, does a lot=
 of venture-type investing in private tech companies=C2=A0without=C2=A0all=
=C2=A0this relationship-building stuff; it just buys stock with money=C2=A0=
and leaves them alone.One way to think about special purpose acquisition co=
mpanies is that they are sort of venture-capital deals to go public. When y=
ou sign a deal with a SPAC, you get cash and a public listing (like you wou=
ld in an IPO), but you also get a long-term relationship with the SPAC=E2=
=80=99s sponsor. The sponsor generally joins your board of directors, typic=
ally along with a couple of other fancy people that the sponsor has signed =
up to be directors. For many young companies, part of the appeal of a SPAC =
deal is that you get a bunch of highly qualified directors all at once, dir=
ectors whom you couldn=E2=80=99t have gotten by just going public and tryin=
g to recruit them. (If you want Shaq on your board, merging with a Shaq SPA=
C is the way to go.) The sponsor will=C2=A0tout her industry connections an=
d financial-structuring expertise and whatever else she brings to the table=
; you=E2=80=99ll sign with her not just for the money she brings but also f=
or the rest of the continuing relationship.Alongside the SPAC merger, a com=
pany will typically raise additional money, privately, in a PIPE deal (=E2=
=80=9Cprivate investment in public equity=E2=80=9D). The PIPE investors wil=
l put in money alongside the SPAC, in order to make the deal bigger and pro=
vide more certainty. (SPAC investors have withdrawal rights, so if you sign=
 a $500 million SPAC deal you can=E2=80=99t be sure of getting $500 million=
; if you throw in a $400 million PIPE alongside the SPAC you=E2=80=99re gua=
ranteed at least the $400 million.) The PIPE investors will often be the so=
rts of big boring institutional investors you=E2=80=99d get in an initial p=
ublic offering, but not necessarily:While most PIPE participants are instit=
utional investors, they sometimes include strategic investors.And that=E2=
=80=99s where Palantir (ticker: PLTR) comes in.To date, Palantir has partic=
ipated in at least eight SPAC-related PIPE transactions, investing well ove=
r $100 million, using the deals as a way to win business from emerging comp=
anies that can benefit from Palantir=E2=80=99s big data analytics software.=
 In effect, Palantir is providing capital up front in return for a multiyea=
r commitments to use the company=E2=80=99s software.Obviously Palantir coul=
d just go buy stock in public companies, on the stock exchange,=C2=A0if it =
wanted to, but it doesn=E2=80=99t; that has none of the relationship-buildi=
ng advantages of this. Or it could invest in venture rounds, if it wanted t=
o, but those are sometimes small and exclusive. A SPAC PIPE is like a ventu=
re round but for an about-to-be-public company.

Inflation indexingA major problem in finance is that a lot of lawyers=C2=A0=
became lawyers because they did not like math, while a lot of bankers and t=
raders became bankers and traders because they did not like to read. So lot=
s of financial contracts will consist of 10 or 50 or 200 pages of text, whi=
ch a lawyer will cheerfully write (or sullenly copy and paste, fine) but wh=
ich her client will not read, and buried within those pages there will be l=
ike three formulas, which the lawyer will write and which might be wrong. T=
he lawyer, who fears math, will write the formula wrong, and her client, wh=
o knows math but fears words, will not read it, and so the wrong formula wi=
ll be enshrined in the contract. (It does not help that the formula will ge=
nerally be=C2=A0written=C2=A0in words =E2=80=94=C2=A0it will look like a ve=
ry long sentence rather than a formula =E2=80=94=C2=A0due mostly to typogra=
phical limitations. So it won=E2=80=99t look appealing to=C2=A0anybody.)=C2=
=A0These are both =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Clawyers can=E2=80=99t do math,=E2=
=80=9D =E2=80=9Ctraders can=E2=80=99t read=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0cruel st=
ereotypes and mostly untrue, so in fact the formulas are rarely wrong. (On =
my old desk, several of the bankers were ex-lawyers, and our lawyer had an =
advanced degree in math.) But not=C2=A0entirely=C2=A0untrue, so sometimes t=
he formulas are wrong. We have talked a  couple of  times about a Ligand Ph=
armaceuticals Inc. convertible bond where, deep in=C2=A0of a document that =
no one read,=C2=A0the lawyers put a constant in a formula where they should=
 have put a variable, with the result that instead of paying $700 million t=
he bond said it would pay $5 billion. Oops! This was plainly a mistake: It =
was not what=C2=A0anyone had agreed to; the formula written in the document=
 did not reflect the economic deal that the parties understood. So Ligand c=
orrected the document, and then some investors sued (you gotta try!), but t=
hey lost. You get to correct obvious mistakes, the judge decided.=C2=A0Or h=
ere=E2=80=99s an even sillier story  from Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Tracy Alloway=
:In this particular contract (a lease for a term of 25 years), Monsolar agr=
eed to pay annual rent to Woden Park that would increase every year accordi=
ng to inflation as measured by the Retail Price Index, or RPI, one of the t=
wo main consumer price indices produced by the U.K.=E2=80=99s Office for Na=
tional Statistics. The initial amount was set at =C2=A315,000 ($20,691) and=
 was governed by a formula for future increases.That formula, apparently co=
nstructed by the landlord himself from examples =E2=80=98available on the i=
nternet,=E2=80=99 looks something like this:Revised rent =3D Previous year=
=E2=80=99s rent x (May RPI for current year)/(May 2013 RPI)Perhaps you can =
see where this is going?Yeah where it=E2=80=99s going is that double-counts=
: You can adjust for inflation by multiplying last year=E2=80=99s rent by t=
he change in the price index since last year, or you can adjust for inflati=
on by multiplying the first year=E2=80=99s rent by the change in the price =
index since inception, but if you adjust by multiplying last year=E2=80=99s=
 rent by the change in the price index since inception then you=E2=80=99re =
sort of compounding twice. (If you cobble together a formula based on examp=
les on the internet, you might find yourself doing everything at once.) =E2=
=80=9CBy the tenant=E2=80=99s calculation, were RPI to increase over the le=
ase term in line with the preceding 20-year average (2.855% per annum) the =
annual rent payable in the final year of the lease would end up being just =
over =C2=A376,000,000 =E2=80=94 a cool $100 million =E2=80=94 and by the en=
d of the 25-year lease an increase of some 507,000%.=E2=80=9DThe tenant sue=
d to change the contract to say what they meant =E2=80=94=C2=A0adjust, once=
, for inflation =E2=80=94=C2=A0and the landlord said, nah man, you signed t=
he contract, pay us our $100 million. The U.K. court sided with the tenant =
because you get to correct obvious mistakes, the landlord appealed, and the=
 appeals court also sided with the tenant because honestly come on we live =
in a society.=C2=A0Two points here. One is that =E2=80=9Chonestly come on w=
e live in a society=E2=80=9D is a good bedrock principle of the common law;=
 you certainly can=E2=80=99t rely on it to protect all of your interests =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t go around signing contracts that you don=E2=80=
=99t mean! =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it tends to soften the blow of the most egreg=
ious cases. It is, however, a principle that requires human intervention an=
d common sense; it is hard to automate. Smart contracts on the blockchain c=
an=E2=80=99t solve this: If code is law, and the code is wrong, oops, you p=
ay the $100 million.The other thing is that I can=E2=80=99t resist quoting =
this passage from the appellate decision, describing the original judge=E2=
=80=99s decision[2]:He proceeded to consider whether it was clear what sch =
6 was objectively intended to mean. =C2=A0 MonSolar=E2=80=99s case was that=
 the drafting mistake should be corrected by reading the Formula as if it r=
ead:=E2=80=9CRevised Rent =C2=A0=3D =C2=A0Original Rent (=C2=A315,000) =C2=
=A0 x=C2=A0 =C2=A0(May RPI for current year) / (May 2013 RPI)=E2=80=9DFanco=
urt J however considered that the appropriate correction was as follows:=E2=
=80=9CRevised Rent =C2=A0=3D =C2=A0Previous year=E2=80=99s Rent =C2=A0 =C2=
=A0x=C2=A0 =C2=A0(May RPI for current year) / (May RPI for previous year)=
=E2=80=9DI will call these =E2=80=9CCorrection A=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9CCorr=
ection B=E2=80=9D respectively. =C2=A0=C2=A0No doubt due to the fact that h=
e was having to resolve the issues on written submissions alone, Fancourt J=
 evidently thought that Correction A and Correction B produced different re=
sults, and he proceeded to explain why he preferred Correction B. =C2=A0Thi=
s part of his judgment, with respect, is not entirely easy to understand, b=
ut in summary it appears he thought that Correction A would only produce si=
mple increases whereas Correction B would produce compound increases. =C2=
=A0It is however common ground that the premise is false and that Correctio=
n A and Correction B will always produce exactly the same results. =C2=A0Th=
is is because RPI itself is in effect compounded, as a simple illustration =
demonstrates: if an item costing =C2=A3100 is subject to RPI increases of 2=
% a year, it will cost =C2=A3102 after one year and =C2=A3104.04 (not =C2=
=A3104) after two years, and the total RPI increase over the two years will=
 be 4.04% not 4%. =C2=A0So it does not matter whether one calculates the pr=
ice at the end of year 2 as =C2=A3100 x 104.04/100 (Correction A), or =C2=
=A3102 x 104.04/102 (Correction B). =C2=A0 Both produce the same figure of =
=C2=A3104.04.Compounding! Who can understand it? If you get it wrong in you=
r contract and go to court to fix it, what are the odds the judge will unde=
rstand it?Things happenGoldman Sachs offices are open, but getting junior b=
ankers to return full time is proving tough. More Credit Suisse Senior Bank=
ers Depart in Wake of Archegos Loss. China to  Sell More Metals From Reserv=
es to Ensure Stable Prices. Bitcoin Fraud Concerns Draw Scrutiny From Regul=
ators. Ever Given Container Ship That Blocked Suez Canal Sets Sail After Ow=
ner Pays Compensation. =E2=80=9COur results suggest that skilled individual=
 investors exploit market inefficiencies (or perhaps conditional risk premi=
ums) to earn abnormal profits.=E2=80=9D Scientists discover spiders are eat=
ing snakes all over the world. The Best  Pizza City in America Is Portland,=
 Oregon. =E2=80=9CHe=E2=80=99s ambivalent about pursuing bank work himself.=
 =E2=80=98Maybe,=E2=80=99 he says. =E2=80=98The thing that=E2=80=99s not in=
teresting is that it goes on all day.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=
=99s like a Gramercy Park for dogs.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CIs this France=E2=80=
=99s most extreme performance artist =E2=80=93 and how does he go to the to=
ilet?=E2=80=9DIf you'd like to get=C2=A0Money=C2=A0Stuff=C2=A0in handy emai=
l form, right in your inbox, please=C2=A0subscribe at this link. Or you can=
 subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here. Thank=
s![1] Another disadvantage is that anyone can sell your stock, including sh=
ort sellers. There is a theoretical view that publicly traded stock should =
be worth more than private stock, because it is more liquid, but there is a=
n argument that private stock is often actually worth more because it=E2=80=
=99s harder to sell.[2] I have made minor typographical alterations due to =
my own typesetting limitations.=C2=A0

5px;">Didi VIEs</h2><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In my old life, I was a de=
rivatives structurer at an investment bank, and an experience that I freque=
ntly had was getting calls like this:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;"><strong>Relationship banker:</strong> Our client wants to do ______.=
</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Me:</strong> That=E2=80=99s illega=
l.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Relationship banker:</strong> Ye=
s that=E2=80=99s our problem.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Me:</=
strong> Ah.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Relationship banker:</s=
trong> We were hoping there was a =E2=80=A6 derivative?</p></blockquote><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Sometimes there was! If you came to me and said =
=E2=80=9Cour client would like to buy 8% of a public company, but not discl=
ose her ownership,=E2=80=9D I would say =E2=80=9Cwell, under U.S. law you d=
o have to disclose that level of stock ownership, but I could put you in a =
nice derivative that gets around that requirement.=E2=80=9D The client coul=
d enter into a swap that gives her economic exposure to the shares, but not=
 actual ownership, so she would not be subject to the laws about ownership =
disclosure. Everyone is happy, more or less. (No one is happy, and we have =
<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24382607.274778/aH=
R0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDQtMDUvYmlsb=
C1od2FuZy1zLWFyY2hlZ29zLXN3YXAtYWZ0ZXJtYXRoLWlzLWF3a3dhcmQtZm9yLWJhbmtz/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB75d6d394" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;"> talked</a> a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/24382607.274778/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlua=
W9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDQtMDgvYXJjaGVnb3MtZ290LXRvby1iaWctZm9yLWl0cy1iYW5r=
cw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBb313a6b0" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"i=
temscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border=
-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: soli=
d; background-color: #ccc;"> few</a> <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/24382607.274778/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=
aW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDQtMjIvanBtb3JnYW4tcy1ldXJvcGVhbi1zdXBlci1sZWFndWU=

 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> times</a=
> about how mad everyone is that Archegos Capital Management=C2=A0was allow=
ed to do this.)</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So I have a soft spot for C=
hinese VIEs. The idea is that, under Chinese law, it is somewhere between =
=E2=80=9Ccomplicated=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cforbidden=E2=80=9D for foreigner=
s to own certain big important Chinese tech=C2=A0companies. This is a probl=
em for those companies if they want to raise capital from foreign investors=
 and list their stocks on foreign stock exchanges. But there is a solution.=
 =E2=80=9COwnership=E2=80=9D of a company is a complicated notion, a vague =
jumble of rights to elect directors and approve mergers and claim a residua=
l interest=C2=A0in the company=E2=80=99s cash flows. You could break those =
things up and sell them separately. Write a profit-sharing contract that sa=
ys =E2=80=9CA will pay B all of A=E2=80=99s profits after expenses for the =
next 100 years, renewable at B=E2=80=99s option,=E2=80=9D and hey that=E2=
=80=99s a residual claim on cash flows. (Or something vaguer: =E2=80=9CA wi=
ll pay B an annual consulting fee that B decides in its total discretion ba=
sed on the economic value of the relationship,=E2=80=9D etc.; not technical=
ly a residual claim but what else is it?) =E2=80=9CB will provide managemen=
t services to A and A will follow B=E2=80=99s instructions,=E2=80=9D hey th=
at=E2=80=99s basically control. =E2=80=9CB will have the right to appoint a=
 majority of A=E2=80=99s board of directors,=E2=80=9D put it in a contract,=
 it=E2=80=99s not actually stock ownership. Etc. Write some contracts that,=
 bundled together, look like ownership, but=C2=A0<em>aren=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</=
em>ownership<em>.</em></p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">With Chinese compani=
es this sort of thing is generally called a =E2=80=9Cvariable interest enti=
ty.=E2=80=9D You set up a company in the Cayman Islands that can be owned b=
y anyone. The Caymans company enters into a series of contracts with the lo=
cal Chinese company, giving it, not ownership, but certain carefully curate=
d economic interests and control rights over the Chinese company. Then you =
list the Caymans company in the U.S., and people buy its stock, and they so=
rt of pretend that they=E2=80=99re buying stock in the Chinese company =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0they sort of pretend that the Chinese company is a=C2=A0<em>sub=
sidiary=C2=A0</em>of the Caymans holding company =E2=80=94=C2=A0even though=
 really they=E2=80=99re only buying an empty shell that has certain contrac=
tual relationships with the Chinese company.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">The problem with this is that it sort of sounds like you=E2=80=99re kiddi=
ng. So here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/243=
82607.274778/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE3NjQ3NT=
cvMDAwMTA0NzQ2OTIxMDAxMjI3L2EyMjQzMzAwejQyNGI0Lmh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a2=
3cB2afb4b0f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc=
;">the prospectus for Didi Global Inc.</a>, a Cayman Islands company that h=
as certain contractual relationships with a giant Chinese ride-hailing comp=
any. (Technically the top-level Chinese company is called Beijing Xiaoju Sc=
ience and Technology Co. Ltd., though colloquially it is =E2=80=9CDidi Chux=
ing.=E2=80=9D) Didi Global did an initial public offering of its American d=
epositary shares last week; it has a market capitalization of something lik=
e $57 billion. Page 12 of the prospectus has a diagram of the corporate str=
ucture, which looks almost normal:</p><!--[if mso]>

<![endif]--><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Looks like a holding company with =
some intermediate holding companies and operating subsidiaries, fine. The o=
nly weird thing is that somewhere near the middle there is a=C2=A0<em>doubl=
e=C2=A0</em>arrow (representing =E2=80=9Ccontractual arrangements=E2=80=9D)=
 rather than a single arrow (representing =E2=80=9Cequity interest=E2=80=9D=
). Didi Global=E2=80=99s shareholders =E2=80=9Cown,=E2=80=9D in some fairly=
 normal sense, everything above the double arrow, right down to one =E2=80=
=9Cwholly foreign owned enterprise=E2=80=9D (WFOE) in China. Everything bel=
ow the double arrow =E2=80=94=C2=A0the actual ride-hailing business, etc. =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0is slightly askew; they just have contractual rights to do s=
tuff with it.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The actual contractual rights=
 are spelled out on <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/clic=
k/24382607.274778/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE3N=

prop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">pages 100-102 =
of the prospectus</a> under the heading =E2=80=9CContractual Arrangements w=
ith Our Variable Interest Entities,=E2=80=9D and they are worth reading. =
=E2=80=9CAgreements that Allow Us to Receive Economic Benefits from Our Var=
iable Interest Entities=E2=80=9D is one sub-heading, describing a contract =
providing that =E2=80=9CBeijing DiDi or its designated parties have the exc=
lusive right to provide Xiaoju Technology with comprehensive technical supp=
ort, consulting services and other services,=E2=80=9D and that =E2=80=9CXia=
oju Technology agrees to pay services fees, the amount of which is determin=
ed by Beijing DiDi on the basis of the work performed and commercial value =
of the services.=E2=80=9D Is that a residual claim on the cash flows of the=
 ride-hailing business? Maybe!</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CAgr=
eements that Provide Us with Effective Control over Our Variable Interest E=
ntities=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9CAgreements that Provide Us with the Option to=
 Purchase the Equity Interest in Our Variable Interest Entities=E2=80=9D ar=
e two other sub-headings. (The latter is less of a stock option and more of=
 a transfer restriction: It=E2=80=99s not that Didi can practically buy the=
 shares in the VIE; it=E2=80=99s that it doesn=E2=80=99t want the sharehold=
ers selling those shares to someone else.) And here=E2=80=99s this:</p><blo=
ckquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><em>Spousal Consent Letters.</em> The =
spouses of the shareholders of Xiaoju Technology have each signed a spousal=
 consent letter agreeing that the equity interests in Xiaoju Technology hel=
d by and registered under the name of the respective shareholders will be d=
isposed pursuant to the contractual agreements with Beijing DiDi. Each spou=
se agreed not to assert any rights over the equity interest in Xiaoju Techn=
ology held by the respective shareholder.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;"><em>Somebody=C2=A0</em>owns the ride-hailing business, in a tec=
hnical legal sense, and it=E2=80=99s not Didi Global or its shareholders. Y=
ou don=E2=80=99t want the actual owners, or their spouses, to go around sel=
ling the shares out from under Didi.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=
=E2=80=99t want to pick on Didi. This is a standard method for mainland Chi=
nese internet companies to go public, Didi is just the latest in a long lin=
e of big companies to use it, the market has come to accept it, and Didi=E2=
=80=99s Chinese counsel has opined that =E2=80=9Cthe ownership structure of=
 our principal variable interest entity ... will not result in any violatio=
n of the applicable PRC laws or regulations currently in effect=E2=80=9D an=
d that =E2=80=9Cthe agreements under the contractual arrangement among Beij=
ing DiDi, Xiaoju Technology and its shareholders are currently valid, bindi=
ng and enforceable in accordance with their terms and the applicable PRC la=
ws or regulations currently in effect.=E2=80=9D It=E2=80=99s all fine!</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But, again, it sounds like you=E2=80=99re kiddi=
ng, and as that legal opinion sort of implies, the laws can change. <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/24382607.274778/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDctMDcvY2hpbmEtbXVsbHMt=
Y2xvc2luZy1sb29waG9sZS11c2VkLWJ5LXRlY2gtZ2lhbnRzLWZvci11LXMtaXBvcw/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cBd39dd136" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;"> So</a>!</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Regul=
ators in Beijing are planning rule changes that would allow them to block a=
 Chinese company from listing overseas even if the unit selling shares is i=
ncorporated outside China, closing a loophole long-used by the country=E2=
=80=99s technology giants, according to people familiar with the matter.</p=
><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The China Securities Regulatory Commission is=
 leading efforts to revise rules on overseas listings that have been in eff=
ect since 1994 and make no reference to companies registered in places like=
 the Cayman Islands, said the people, asking not to be identified discussin=
g a private matter. Once amended, the rules would require firms structured =
using the so-called Variable Interest Entity model to seek approval before =
going public in Hong Kong or the U.S., the people said.=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The proposed change is the first indication of how Bei=
jing plans to implement a crackdown on overseas listings flagged by the cou=
ntry=E2=80=99s State Council on Tuesday. Closer oversight would plug a gap =
that=E2=80=99s been used for two decades by technology giants from Alibaba =
Group Holding Ltd. to Tencent Holdings Ltd. to attract foreign capital and =
list offshore, potentially thwarting the ambitions of firms like ByteDance =
Ltd. contemplating going public outside the mainland. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Pioneered by Sina Corp. and its investment bankers dur=
ing a 2000 initial public offering, the VIE framework has never been formal=
ly endorsed by Beijing. It has nevertheless enabled Chinese companies to si=
destep restrictions on foreign investment in sensitive sectors including th=
e Internet industry. The structure allows a Chinese firm to transfer profit=
s to an offshore entity -- registered in places like the Cayman Islands or =
the British Virgin Islands -- with shares that foreign investors can then o=
wn.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While virtually every major Chinese int=
ernet company has used the structure, it=E2=80=99s become increasingly worr=
isome for Beijing as it tightens its grip on technology firms that have inf=
iltrated every corner of Chinese life and control reams of consumer data. A=
uthorities so far have little legal recourse to prevent sensitive overseas =
listings, as with the recent Didi Global Inc. IPO, which went ahead despite=
 requests for a delay from regulators.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The =
additional oversight could bestow a level of legitimacy on the VIE structur=
e that=E2=80=99s been a perennial worry for global investors given the shak=
y legal ground on which it stands.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px=
 0;">Yeah that=E2=80=99s not the worst outcome. The worst outcome would be =
something like the Chinese government declaring =E2=80=9Call these VIE cont=
racts are actually a disguised form of foreign ownership, which is not allo=
wed by the rules, so they are all void and your Didi and Alibaba shares are=
 worthless.=E2=80=9D That would =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I mean, these VIE contracts=
=C2=A0<em>are=C2=A0</em>a disguised form of foreign ownership, and a fairly=
 thin disguise. (It=E2=80=99s not like Chinese government authorities are <=
em>unaware</em> that foreign investors own shares in Alibaba; it=E2=80=99s =
just that they seem to accept this level of technical compliance with the r=
ules.) This is a real risk! It=E2=80=99s in <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bl=
oombergbusiness.com/click/24382607.274778/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoa=
XZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE3NjQ3NTcvMDAwMTA0NzQ2OTIxMDAxMjI3L2EyMjQzMzAwejQyNGI0=
Lmh0bSNkYTEwMjAxX3Jpc2tfZmFjdG9ycw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBc2e731eb" itemp=
rop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Didi=E2=80=99s =
risk factors</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">PRC laws and =
regulations impose restrictions on foreign ownership and investment in cert=
ain internet-based businesses. We are an exempted company incorporated in t=
he Cayman Islands and our PRC subsidiaries are considered foreign-invested =
enterprises. To comply with PRC laws, regulations and regulatory requiremen=
ts, we set up a series of contractual arrangements entered into among some =
of our PRC subsidiaries, our VIEs and their shareholders to conduct some of=
 our operations in China. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We have been =
further advised by our PRC legal counsel that there are substantial uncerta=
inties regarding the interpretation and application of current or future PR=
C laws and regulations. Thus, the PRC government may ultimately take a view=
 contrary to the opinion of our PRC legal counsel. If the PRC government ot=
herwise find that we are in violation of any existing or future PRC laws or=

ess, the relevant governmental authorities would have broad discretion in d=
ealing with such violation, including, without limitation:=C2=A0=E2=80=A6</=
p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">revoking the business licenses and/or operat=
ing licenses of our PRC entities; ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">requi=
ring us to restructure our ownership structure or operations, including ter=
minating the contractual arrangements with our VIEs and deregistering the e=
quity pledges of our VIEs, which in turn would affect our ability to consol=
idate, derive economic interests from, or exert effective control over our =
VIEs and their subsidiaries; ...</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">If the Chinese government decides =E2=80=9CVIEs are okay, but you have t=

nes already listed abroad were just, you know,=C2=A0<em>voided</em>.</p><p =

ttom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px 5px;">Palantir SPACs</h2=
><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The basic deal, when a company goes public, i=
s that anyone can buy its stock. You put out a notice saying =E2=80=9Chey w=
e have stock for sale, please buy it,=E2=80=9D and then whoever is willing =
to pay the most buys the stock. The advantage of this, for the company, is =
generally that you=C2=A0can raise a lot of money. Deep liquid public market=
s are good for valuation; people are more willing to take a risk on buying =
stock that they can easily resell. So if you want money, having public stoc=
k is good. The disadvantage is that anyone can buy your stock. Activist inv=
estors can buy your stock and yell at you to change things. Hostile bidders=
 can tender for your stock to try to take you over. Crazy retail investors =
can push your stock around. Index funds can buy your stock and sit around d=
oing nothing. Etc.[1]</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is another mode=
l, in which you interview=C2=A0the people who want to buy your stock and ch=
oose the best ones. Price matters =E2=80=94=C2=A0people who will pay more f=
or your stock are better for you than people who will pay less =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but it is not the only thing. Buying your stock is a way to start a rela=
tionship, and you will try to sell stock to people who will be good relatio=
nship partners. Wise investors who can give you good advice will be more ap=
pealing than hands-off index funds. Hands-off index funds will be more appe=
aling than mean activists who will boss you around and try to fire your man=
agers. Selling stock to your competitors seems bad. Selling stock to your s=
uppliers or customers to cement your business relationships seems good.</p>=

sal, in venture-capital-funded private companies. VCs advertise themselves =

uppliers. This is so standard in venture capital that it is <a href=3D"http=

 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">While most PIPE participants are institutional i=

: 16px 0;">And that=E2=80=99s where Palantir (ticker: PLTR) comes in.</p><p=

 is providing capital up front in return for a multiyear commitments to use=
 the company=E2=80=99s software.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">Obviously Palantir could just go buy stock in public companies, on the s=
